coredata.dc:title: Smartphone assisted fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ and ascorbic acid determination based on off-on carbon dots probe
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.

coredata.dc:creator: XU, Guan-Ting
ZHAO, Tian-Sheng
ZHANG, Kun
GUO, Li-Zhen
HE, Yong-Qin
HU, Jian-Hua
LIAO, Yi-Jing
MAI, Xi
LI, Na
coredata.dc:description: Iron ions (Fe3+) and ascorbic acid (AA) are of great significance to living organisms. Herein, carbon dots (C-dots)-based “off-on” fluorescent probe was designed for Fe3+ and AA analysis. In presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence of C-dots was immediately quenched, and the quenching mechanism was deduced to be inner filter effect (IFE). The fluorescent C-dot probe shows response to Fe3+ in range of 10–500 µM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.9 µM. Interestingly, when AA was introduced to the C-dots-Fe3+ system, the fluorescence of C-dots was significantly recovered, which could be ascribed to the oxidation-reduction reaction between Fe3+ and AA. Based “off-on” fluorescent C-dots probe, the linear range of 5–500 µM was obtained with LOD of 0.4 µM. Moreover, the AA-induced fluorescence enhancement can be recognized by smartphone, which greatly extends the application in resource-limited areas.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fe3+
Ascorbic acid
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quantification of Fe3+ and AA
Detection of AA in real samples
The feasibility and mechanism of the sensor for Fe3+ and AA analysis
Response of the C-dots for Fe3+
Response of the C-dots/Fe3+ for AA
Analysis of AA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of smartphone assisted fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ and AA determination based on “off-on” carbon dots probe.

(A) Absorption, fluorescence excitation (dash dot line), and emission (solid line) spectra of C-dots. Insets: pictures taken under visible (i) and 365 nm UV (ii) light. (B) Excitation-emission matrices of C-dots. (C) TEM image of C-dots and (D) the distribution of particle size.

(A) UV-vis absorption spectrum of Fe3+ (dash dot line) and fluorescence (FL) excitation spectrum of C-dots (solid line). (B) Time-resolved fluorescence spectra of C-dots before and after addition of 0.5 and 1.0 mM Fe3+, respectively.
Optimization of AA detection conditions. Effects of Fe3+ concentration (A, B), pH (C), and incubation time (D) on AA detection.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the C-dots with different Fe3+ concentrations (0–3 mM). (B) Fluorescence intensities and linear relationship of ΔF/F 0 (inset) versus Fe3+concentration. (C) The responses of C-dots to Fe3+ and some other metal ions. (D) Fluorescence spectra of the C-dots-Fe3+ system with different AA concentrations (0–5 mM). (E) Fluorescence intensities and linear relationship of ΔF/F 0 (inset) versus AA concentration. (F) The responses of C-dots-Fe3+ system to AA and some other potential interference substances.
Screen captures (A) and RGB figure analysis (B) by the Swatches App of iPhone 13. (C) Linear relationship of B versus AA concentration (0 – 150 µM).
Determination of AA in vitamin C tablets (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Smartphone assisted fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ and ascorbic acid determination based on off-on carbon dots probe

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Iron ions (Fe3+) and ascorbic acid (AA) are of great significance to living organisms. Herein, carbon dots (C-dots)-based “off-on” fluorescent probe was designed for Fe3+ and AA analysis. In presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence of C-dots was immediately quenched, and the quenching mechanism was deduced to be inner filter effect (IFE). The fluorescent C-dot probe shows response to Fe3+ in range of 10–500 µM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.9 µM. Interestingly, when AA was introduced to the C-dots-Fe3+ system, the fluorescence of C-dots was significantly recovered, which could be ascribed to the oxidation-reduction reaction between Fe3+ and AA. Based “off-on” fluorescent C-dots probe, the linear range of 5–500 µM was obtained with LOD of 0.4 µM. Moreover, the AA-induced fluorescence enhancement can be recognized by smartphone, which greatly extends the application in resource-limited areas.

ce:section.ce:para: Many diseases are difficult to be diagnosed at early stage, because their related biomarkers are at low level [1]. Thus, it is essential to develop sensitive and point-of-care (POC) sensors for biomarker detection [2,3]. As is reported, naked-eye POC testing (POCT) can realize qualitative analysis [4,5]. However, qualitative and quantitative POCT is still confronted with huge challenges. Meanwhile, it is difficult to diagnose the disease based on an only biomarker. Testing the level of multiple biomarkers is superior to a single one in predicting diseases [6]. Iron ions (Fe3+) and ascorbic acid (AA) are indispensable to living organisms, which are widely involved in various physiological processes [7]. For example, the reactive oxygen species (ROS) can be formed from Fe3+-catalyzed degradation of AA [8]. Therefore, it is of great significance to achieve a simple method for detections of both Fe3+ and AA, which helps individuals to monitor important biomarkers and prevent diseases.

Up to mow, various techniques have been reported for Fe3+ and AA analysis, such as chromatography [9], electrochemistry [10,11], ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption [12–15], fluorescence [16–19], and chemiluminescence [20–23]. Among these techniques, the fluorescence method is noted for its high sensitivity, selectivity, as well as accuracy. The fluorescent probe acts an essential role in these fluorescence methods, which commonly shows the “off/on” response to targets. Recently, nano-material based fluorescent probes attracted intensive attention because of their superior fluorescence properties [24,25]. As a representative, the carbon dots (C-dots) have been widely employed as fluorescent nano-probes to develop analytical methods [26–28]. To obtain C-dots with high quantum yields, doping with heteroatoms is one of the most effective methods to tailor the fluorescent properties of C-dots [29]. Moreover, expanding the synthesized C-dots to novel applications is becoming more and more significant.

Recently, there have been researches focused on Fe3+ and AA analysis by employing C-dots as fluorescent probes. However, most of these researches cannot realize quantitative analysis without employment of fluorescence spectrophotometer, which greatly limits the application in resource-limited areas. Thus, portable and economic device for reading fluorescence is in need. Smartphone, known as the tool for communication, has been reported as a handy digital process center, which can act as a transducer [30–32]. When a smartphone is installed with the corresponding application (App), the camera of smartphone can take the photos of samples and the App can output the numerical values of the photos. Therefore, with the help of the installed software, smartphone can be utilized to recognize target-induced color change.

Herein, we reported a smartphone assisted fluorescent sensor by using “off-on” C-dots probe for Fe3+ and AA determination. The C-dots feature strong blue fluorescence at around 420 nm in aqueous solution, which can be selectively quenched by Fe3+. Furthermore, the as-synthesized C-dots can be employed for AA detection based on an “off-on” fluorescence response. As shown in Scheme 1 , the fluorescence intensity is greatly decreased after addition of Fe3+ due to the of inner filter effect (IFE). However, after the introduction of AA to the C-dots-Fe3+ solution, the fluorescence of C-dots is significantly recovered, which can be attributed to the oxidation-reduction reaction between Fe3+ and AA. Moreover, the AA-induced fluorescence enhancement can be recognized with the smartphone-based portable device. With the help of App, the RGB values can be obtained for indicating the concentration of AA.

Ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid (CA), sucrose (Suc), glucose (Gluc), and lactose (Lac) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glutamic acid (Glu), glutamine (Gln), and glutathione (GSH) were provided by Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All metal salts FeCl3, NaCl, KCl, MgCl2·6H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, CaCl2·3H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O Cu(NO3)·3H2O, and HgNO3·H2O were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and Titan Scientific Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Vitamin C tablets were purchased from a local pharmacy in Nanchang, China. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used in the experiment.

Fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-2700 spectrofluorophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) with the slits (E x/E m) of 10.0/10.0 nm and the photomultiplier tube (PMT) voltage of 400 V. UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Hitachi UH5300 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime decay spectra were recorded on an FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were done on a JEOL-2100 TEM operated at 200 kV (Japan).

The synthesis route of C-dots is designed based on our previous work with slight modification [33,34]. At first, 7.5 mL of adenosine (20 mM) was mixed with 7.5 mL of PPi (40 mM). The above solution was put into a 25 mL of Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave before placing in a thermostatic oven (150°C) for 6 h. Subsequently, the pale yellow solution was collected and dialyzed against water through a dialysis film (1000 MWCO) for 48 h. The obtained C-dot solution was diluted with ultrapure water to 30 mL and then stored at 4°C in a refrigerator before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantification of Fe3+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Fe3+ and AA was carried out in 10 mM NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 5.0) at room temperature. For Fe3+ detection, 10 µL of C-dot solution (20%, v/v) was mixed with and 50 µL of different concentrations of Fe3+ solution and 440 µL of NaAc-HAc buffer. The mixture was shaken thoroughly and incubated for 2 min. The resulting solution was measured with excitation at 350 nm.

For AA sensing, 50 µL of Fe3+ (15 mM) was added to 10 µL of C-dot solution (20%, v/v) to form a new detection platform, which was denoted as C-dots/Fe3+. Thereafter, 50 µL of different concentrations of AA solution and 390 µL of NaAc-HAc buffer were mixed with the C-dots/Fe3+ solution. After incubation for 30 min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The vitamin C tablets were grinded, blended together, and dissolved in ultrapure water. Filter heads (0.22 µm pore size) were utilized to remove the insoluble impurities. The resulting filtrate was further centrifuged to obtain pure samples. The supernate was diluted to match the range of linearity. The following detection procedures were same as the above. For recovery experiments, different amounts of AA were added to vitamin C powder before the detection of AA.

The optical properties of C-dots were first characterized by UV-vis spectrophotometer (Fig. 1 A). The absorption peak situated at around 340 nm can be ascribed to n→π* transition, indicating the existence of heteroatom-containing double bonds, such as C=O, C=N, and N=N [35]. Thereafter, the fluorescence properties of C-dots were characterized by fluorescence (FL) spectrophotometer. Fig. 1A and B show the maximum excitation and emission of C-dots are 350 and 420 nm, respectively. As can be seen from the inset of Fig. 1A, the synthesized C-dot solution is light yellow under daylight and can emit bright blue fluorescence under the 365 nm UV light. The morphology of synthesized C-dots was characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1C, D, the C-dots are nearly spherical in shape, and about 2.5 nm in diameter, suggesting the successful synthesis of C-dots. As reported in our previous work [33,34], the hydrothermally synthesized N/P co-doping C-dots exhibited high quantum yields, excellent stability, and water solubility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility and mechanism of the sensor for Fe3+ and AA analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of the C-dots-based sensor, the fluorescence of C-dots was investigated under different circumstances. As shown in Fig. S1, the C-dot solution shows strong fluorescence (curve a). After the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence of C-dots is significantly quenched (curve b). However, after AA was introduced to the C-dots-Fe3+ system, the fluorescence of C-dots is largely recovered (curve c). The addition of AA or Fe2+ to C-dot solution does not influence the fluorescence of C-dots (curve d, e). When Fe3+ was added to the C-dots-AA system, the quenching of C-dots is observed (curve f), which might attributed to the existence of excess Fe3+ after reduced by AA. Fig. S1 confirms that C-dots can selectively respond to Fe3+ when compared to Fe2+. Thus, a universal sensing platform for Fe3+ reductant can be constructed. As shown in Fig. S2, Fe3+ can be reduced to Fe2+ by AA, which makes it feasible that the C-dots-Fe3+ probe can be applied to AA sensing.

The possible quenching mechanism between C-dots and Fe3+ was studied. The optical properties of Fe3+ and C-dots probe were first measured (Fig. 2 A). The Fe3+ solution shows strong absorption from 250 to 400 nm, which greatly overlaps with the excitation spectrum of C-dots. Therefore, inner filter effect (IFE) was considered as the main quenching mechanism. To further confirm the IFE-induced fluorescence quenching, fluorescence lifetime experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. 2B and Table S1, the fluorescence lifetime of C-dots keeps almost invariable after 0.5 and 1.0 mM Fe3+ are added, respectively. As IFE-induced fluorescence quenching does not influence the excited state of the fluorophore, the fluorescence lifetime will not change. Thus, the Fe3+-induced fluorescence quenching can be ascribed to IFE.

To obtain optimum experimental conditions for Fe3+ and AA detection, different experimental parameters are optimized and analyzed, including the reaction time, the concentration of Fe3+ for AA detection, and pH of the buffer solution. Firstly, the response of C-dots for Fe3+ was investigated. As can be seen in Fig. S3, the fluorescence of C-dots is immediately quenched with the addition of Fe3+ and keeps stable after 2 min. Thus, after the Fe3+ was added for 2 min, the fluorescence of C-dots was detected. Then, the concentration of Fe3+ was optimized for AA detection. The fluorescence of C-dots is gradually reduced with increase addition of Fe3+ before and after the addition of AA (Fig. 3 A). The sensitivity of the AA sensor is enhanced when the addition of Fe3+ is increased (Fig. 3B). To achieve high sensitivity and balance wide linear range, 1.5 mM Fe3+ is chosen for subsequent experiments. The pH value of reaction buffer solution ranging from 3.5 to 6.0 was also considered (Fig. 3C). The optimal response is obtained with NaAc/HAc buffer (10 mM, pH 5.0). Finally, the reaction kinetics of the system was explored (Fig. 3D). The respond of C-dots/Fe3+ tends to stabilize after 30 min. Overall, the optimal detection conditions are obtained as reaction time for Fe3+ (2 min), Fe3+ (1.5 mM), NaAc/HAc buffer solution (pH 5.0), and reaction time for AA (30 min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of the C-dots for Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions, the fluorescence response of C-dots for Fe3+ detection was explored. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence of C-dots is gradually quenched with the addition of increasing Fe3+. The fluorescence intensity at 420 nm of the sensing system is decreased with increase of Fe3+ from 0 to 3 mM (Fig. 4B). The fluorescence intensity ratio (ΔF/F 0) shows favorable linear relationship with Fe3+ concentration (Fig. 4B, inset). The linear range is obtained as 10 – 500 µM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.9 µM. Herein, the LOD is calculated based on 3σ/slope, where σ is the standard deviation of the 11 blank samples. The LOD obtained in this work is comparable with that of other reported fluorescence probes (Table S2), which demonstrates that the synthesized C-dots can be utilized for efficient Fe3+ detection.

To evaluate the feasibility of the sensor when applied to complex real samples, the selectivity studies of C-dots for Fe3+ were performed. Some metal ions, including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cu2+, and Hg2+, were chosen to investigate their influence on C-dots. As exhibited in Fig. 4C, when the above metal ions are added to the C-dot solution, no obvious change is observed except that Cu2+ shows a little fluorescence quenching. Overall, the addition of Fe3+ can effectively quench the florescence of C-dots, which demonstrate the potential application of C-dots for detecting Fe3+ in complex samples.

The real application of this proposed method was further confirmed by detecting Fe3+ in river samples by a standard addition method. The water samples were collected from the Ganjiang River (Nanchang, China), which were centrifuged and filtered with filter heads (0.22 µm pore size) to remove the impurities. As listed in Table S3, the recoveries of Fe3+ in spiked water samples were varied from 91.96% to 95.98% with relative standard deviation (RSD) below 2%, revealing a reliable and practicable method for Fe3+ detection in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of the C-dots/Fe3+ for AA
ce:section.ce:para: Through the above selectivity investigation, the C-dots show much stronger response for Fe3+ compared to Fe2+, which enables the construction of C-dots/Fe3+ system for AA detection. As shown in Fig. 4D, Fe3+ can be reduced to Fe2+ by AA. Thus, the quenched fluorescence of C-dots/Fe3+ system can be recovered with the addition of AA. Herein, a “turn on” fluorescence sensor is established for AA detection. As shown in Fig. 4E, the fluorescence of C-dots/Fe3+ system is gradually increased with increasing addition of AA (0 – 5 mM). A satisfied linear relationship is achieved between the fluorescence intensity ratio (ΔF/F 0) and 5 – 500 µM AA (Fig. 4E, inset). The calculated LOD of C-dots/Fe3+ complex for AA is determined as 0.4 µM, which is lower than the concentration of AA in biological samples. When compared with some reported carbon-based nanomaterial sensors (Table S2), the newly developed method shows a wide detection range, low detection limit, and can also detect Fe3+ and AA at the same time.

To investigate the anti-interference ability of the developed sensor, the selectivity studies of C-dots/Fe3+ system for AA was carried out. Some potential interference substances in biological samples and drugs was explored under the same experimental conditions, including some reductants (glutathione (GSH), citric acid (CA)), amino acids (glutamic acid (Glu), glutamine (Gln)), and saccharides (glucose (Gluc), sucrose (Suc), and lactose (Lac)). As shown in Fig. 4F, all these potential interfering substances, even GSH, a reductive agent, exhibits no obvious effect on the fluorescence of C-dots/Fe3+ system. The confirmed high selectivity provides the developed AA sensor possibility for application in complex real samples.

For developing a portable tool for AA sensing, color identification App installed smartphone and 365 nm UV lamp installed lucifugal device were employed. The captured images of the samples could be processed in the RGB color space by a smartphone App (Swatches). As shown in Fig. 5 A, the samples exhibit color change from dark to blue with increased AA. The color signals were converted to the blue (B) values (Fig. 5B). The figures of B increase with increased AA, and show favorable linear correlations with concentrations of AA (0 – 150 µM, Fig. 5C). Compared to the naked-eye assisted qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis, smartphone-based analytical method can realize both qualitative and quantitative analysis without the employment of instrument. However, the obtained sensitivity herein is 6.7 µM, which should be further improved to achieve instrument-grade quality. The developed smartphone-based sensor can be applied for in-field AA determination, which can be further confirmed by fluorescent methods in the lab.

The stability of the C-dots is crucial to the establishment of the sensing platform. The fluorescence of C-dots remains well after three months, which suggests good stability of the synthesized C-dots (Fig. S4). Considering the “on-off-on” fluorescence conversion of the C-dots mediated by Fe3+ and AA, the recyclability of the developed sensor was investigated. The fluorescence of C-dots is quenched by Fe3+, and recovered with addition of AA in four cycles (Fig. S5). The proposed sensor that can repeatedly detect Fe3+ and AA at least four times shows good reversibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The content of AA in Vitamin C tablet was determined to assess the feasibility of developed sensor in real samples. The experiments were performed by the standard addition method, and the results were summarized in Table 1 . The contents of AA in Vitamin C tablets were calculated to be 97.39 and 101.26 mg/tablet, which were close to the specification of 100 mg/tablet. Thereafter, 50 and 100 mg/tablet AA were added to the vitamin C samples, respectively. The obtained recovery ranges from 96.06% to 100.67% with RSD of 0.66% – 3.02%. These results indicate that the simple sensing method provided a potential prospect for AA determination in practical samples.

Smartphone assisted fluorescent sensor has been successfully established for Fe3+ and AA determination based on “off-on” C-dots probe. Specifically, “off” fluorescence signal can be observed when Fe3+ was added to C-dots. On the contrary, fluorescence signal can be turned “on” when AA was added to the C-dots-Fe3+ system. The LOD for Fe3+ and AA were obtained as low as 0.9 and 0.4 µM, respectively. The one-pot synthesis approach of C-dots was simple, and the detection process is easy to operate. With high sensitivity, simplicity, and rapid signal readout, we believe this smartphone-assisted method provide insights into the development of new portable sensors for biochemical detections.

Guan-Ting XU: Conceptualization, Investigation. Tian-Sheng ZHAO: Writing – original draft. Kun ZHANG: Validation. Li-Zhen GUO: Data curation. Yong-Qin HE: Formal analysis. Jian-Hua HU: Project administration. Yi-Jing LIAO: Methodology. Xi MAI: Writing – review & editing. Na LI: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81860610).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cjac.2022.100206.
